data.title: OPEC+ set to discuss July oil output hike, may be larger than 411,000 bpd, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • OPEC+ may discuss hike larger than 411,000 bpd, sources say
  • Eight OPEC+ members to meet online on Saturday at 0900 GMT
  • Kazakhstan has informed OPEC it won't cut output
  • For an EXPLAINER on OPEC+ output cutsclick here

OPEC+ meets on Saturday to discuss an increase in oil output for July that may be larger than the 411,000 barrels per day (bpd) increases it made for May and June, sources familiar with OPEC+ talks told Reuters.

Eight OPEC+ countries have been raising output more rapidly than earlier planned, even though the extra supply has weighed on prices. The strategy of group leaders Saudi Arabia and Russia is aimed partly at punishing over-producing allies and to win back market share, Reuters has reported.

The eight members, set to meet online at 0900 GMT, could discuss an increase larger than 411,000 bpd for July, two sources familiar with OPEC+ talks and two OPEC+ delegates said. They could also keep the hike at 411,000 bpd, other sources said.

All sources declined to be identified by name due to the sensitivity of the matter. OPEC and authorities in Russia and Saudi Arabia did not respond to requests for comment sent on Friday.

Kazakhstan's statement on Thursday that it won't cut production has sparked debate in OPEC+, some of the sources said on Friday, with one saying that this factor may tilt discussions towards a larger output hike on Saturday.

While eight leading OPEC+ members are increasing supply, some of those are being asked to temper those increases to compensate for producing more than their monthly quotas. Kazakhstan's statement suggested it may not do so.

"Kazakhstan's repeated public displays of production defiance do raise the risk of an even bigger output increase," said Helima Croft of RBC Capital Markets, adding that she still saw a 411,000 bpd hike for July as likely.

Kazakhstan has been pumping far above its OPEC+ target, a factor that has angered other OPEC+ members and helped prompt the group to proceed with plans to hike output beginning in April, sources said at the time.

United Arab Emirates Energy Minister Suhail Mohamed Al Mazrouei, asked on Tuesday about the output plan for July, said OPEC+ was doing its best to balance the oil market.

Oil prices fell to a four-year low in April, slipping below $60 per barrel after OPEC+ said it was tripling its output hike in May and as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs raised concerns about global economic weakness. Prices closed just below $63 on Friday.

OPEC+ includes OPEC members and allies such as Russia. Output increases that began in April are aimed at unwinding some 2.2 million bpd of voluntary output cuts by eight leading member states.

data.title: India says changed tactics worked well in conflict with Pakistan
data.description:
Key points:
  • India switched tactics after initial air losses, says general
  • Ceasefire declared after intense conflict involving jets and missiles
  • No nuclear threat during conflict, Chauhan says

India switched tactics after suffering losses in the air on the first day of conflict with Pakistan earlier this month and established a decisive advantage before the neighbours announced a ceasefire three days later, India's highest ranking general said on Saturday.

The heaviest fighting in decades between nuclear-armed India and Pakistan was sparked by an April 22 attack in Indian Kashmir that killed 26 people, most of them tourists. New Delhi blamed the incident on "terrorists" backed by Pakistan, a charge denied by Islamabad.

On May 7, Indian jets bombed what New Delhi called "terrorist infrastructure" sites across the border. Pakistan has said it downed six Indian planes, including at least three Rafale fighters, in the initial clashes.

The ceasefire was announced on May 10 after bitter fighting in which both sides used fighter jets, missiles, drones and artillery.

General Anil Chauhan, India's chief of defence staff, said in an interview that India suffered initial losses in the air, but declined to give details.

"What was important is, why did these losses occur, and what we'll do after that," he told Reuters on the sidelines of the Shangri-La Dialogue security forum in Singapore, referring to the Pakistani claim of downing jets.

"So we rectified tactics and then went back on the 7th, 8th and 10th in large numbers to hit air bases deep inside Pakistan, penetrated all their air defences with impunity, carried out precision strikes."

The Indian air force "flew all types of aircraft with all types of ordinances on the 10th", he said.

India has previously said its missiles and drones struck at least eight Pakistani air bases across the country that day, including one near the capital Islamabad.

The Pakistan military says that India did not fly its fighter jets again in the conflict after suffering losses on May 7.

India's director general of air operations, Air Marshal A.K. Bharti, had told a press conference earlier in the month that "losses are a part of combat" and that India had downed some Pakistani jets.

Islamabad has denied it suffered any losses of planes but has acknowledged its air bases suffered some hits although losses were minimal.

NO NUCLEAR WORRIES

Some of the attacks were on bases near Pakistan's nuclear facilities, but they themselves were not targeted, media reports have said.

"Most of the strikes were delivered with pinpoint accuracy, some even to a metre, to whatever was our selected mean point of impact," Chauhan said.

Chauhan, and Pakistan's chairman of the joint chiefs of staff, Gen. Sahir Shamshad Mirza, have both said there was no danger at any time during the conflict that nuclear weapons were considered.

"I think there's a lot of space before that nuclear threshold is crossed, a lot of signalling before that, I think nothing like that happened," Chauhan said. "There's a lot of space for conventional operations which has been created, and this will be the new norm.

"It's my personal view that the most rational people are people in uniform when conflict takes place," he added. "During this operation, I found both sides displaying a lot of rationality in their thoughts as well as actions. So why should we assume that in the nuclear domain there will be irrationality on someone else's part?"

Chauhan also said that although Pakistan is closely allied with China, which borders India in the north and east, there was no sign of any actual help from Beijing during the conflict.

"While this was unfolding from (April) 22nd onwards, we didn't find any unusual activity in the operational or tactical depth of our northern borders, and things were generally all right."

Asked whether China may have provided any satellite imagery or other real-time intelligence to Pakistan during the conflict, Chauhan said such imagery was commercially available and could have been procured from China as well as other sources.

He added that while hostilities had ceased, the Indian government had made it clear it would "respond precisely and decisively should there be any further terror attacks emanating from Pakistan."

"So that has its own dynamics as far the armed forces are concerned. It will require us to be prepared 24/7."

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

Ambarella's raised full-year sales outlook and improved results in its latest quarter appear to be falling short with investors as the stock drops. Investors could be concerned with management's seemingly conservative view toward the second half of the fiscal year after widening the company's guidance range, citing uncertain geopolitical conditions. Management said the upper half of the company's wider guidance range still implies regular seasonality and stronger growth in the back of the year, though. "So, it's really your call. We're just saying, it's an uncertain environment. It could happen and play out a lot of different ways," VP of Corporate Development Louis Gerhardy said during the company's earnings call. Shares fall 18% to $50.62. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1343 GMT - U.S. President Trump's tightening of immigration controls could present a headwind to digital assets, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. "For tech investors and digital asset markets--where the free movement of global talent is core--such moves might presage constraints in the future, impacting innovation cycles and digital asset flows." That comes amid Trump's expanding foray into cryptocurrencies, including the establishment of a strategic bitcoin reserve. Trump's efforts have improved bitcoin's mainstream adoption but also bring ethical and regulatory issues, he says. Bitcoin last trades down 0.6% at $105,578 after hitting a 10-day low of $104,648 earlier, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1259 GMT - Zscaler's latest quarterly report, in which it topped analyst estimates and raised guidance, represents another breakout moment for the cybersecurity stalwart amid a customer shift toward cloud-based security solutions, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. Zscaler is seeing strong demand for its zero trust security platform and its AI security solutions, which will be well received by Wall Street in a choppy spending environment, the analysts say. The company is scoring new clients and reeling in more customers that provide $1 million or more in annual recurring revenue, they say, maintaining an outperform rating on the stock and moving their price target up to $300 from $240. Shares rise 4.9% to $263.50. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1218 GMT - Marvell Technology's lack of data center upside in 1Q and its 2Q guidance leaves more questions about the current ramp of the artificial intelligence business, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The chip maker logged adjusted EPS of 62 cents and revenue of $1.9 billion in 1Q, compared to expectations of 61 cents a share in adjusted EPS and revenue of $1.88 billion. Marvell guided for adjusted EPS between 62 cents and 72 cents and sales of $2 billion, while the Street expected 66 cents a share in adjusted EPS and sales of $1.98 billion. "In short, without data center and AI upside in the year, the stock is a 'show-me story' and may have trouble breaking out," the analysts say. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1206 GMT - Marvell Technology's latest results may have fallen short of some investors' lofty expectations but the company still has plenty of tailwinds, Benchmark's Cody Acree says in a research note. The chip maker's modest upside results and guidance compared to the standout performance by Nvidia is weighing on its stock. But Acree notes that on a comparative basis, the two companies are seeing similar trends with broad based demand strength in AI driving data center growth in both compute and networking. While Acree understands investor's caution, he's encouraged by a few factors including Marvell's market leading interconnect portfolio and the broad cyclical recovery it's seeing in both its carrier infrastructure and enterprise networking business, which he expects to be a positive catalyst for sentiment and its shares. Shares decline 3.4% to $61.59 in premarket trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0956 GMT - Xiaomi's strong momentum across all business segments is likely to continue this year, Bernstein analysts write in a note. The company's 1Q performance was impressive across the board, thanks to significant gains in smartphone market share in China and robust growth in Internet-of-Things demand, they say. Xiaomi is well-positioned to benefit from China's subsidy policies in both the smartphone and IoT sectors due to its product portfolio, they say. Looking ahead, the brokerage raised its revenue forecast by 4% for this year and 3% for 2026. Bernstein reiterates the outperform rating for the company and raises its target price to HK$60.00 from HK$55.00. Shares last closed at HK$50.95. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1347 GMT - The consumer-health industry could see more merger-and-acquisition activity, but smaller deals for certain brands or segments seem more likely than large-scale takeovers, Citi analysts say in a research note. Recent spinoffs such as Kenvue and Haleon, potential splits or asset reviews at Bayer and Reckitt Benckiser, and acquisition attempts like Unilever's for Haleon in 2022 left investors wondering whether the industry could consolidate, Citi says. Healthy balance sheets and interest from the likes of Procter & Gamble suggest there is room for acquisitions, but antitrust concerns and diverse portfolios make large deals unlikely, Citi says. In a large takeover, the buyer would probably need a significant portfolio reshaping afterward, the analysts say. The Stoxx Europe 600 Personal & Household Goods index is up 0.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1258 GMT - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0855 GMT - Sanofi shares fall in European morning trading, while Regeneron Pharmaceuticals is sharply lower in U.S. premarket trading, after the companies reported mixed results from two late-stage trials of a respiratory drug they are jointly developing. Sanofi and Regeneron said drug candidate Itepekimab missed the key goal of a phase 3 trial, but met the primary objective in the other study. Paris-listed shares in Sanofi fall 5.8%, while Regeneron shares drop 12% in U.S. premarket trading. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1914 GMT - Crude futures end the day and the week lower as the market awaits the outcome of Saturday's OPEC+ meeting where the group is expected to decide on another large unwind of voluntary output cuts for July. "Prices continue to consolidate sideways, but there has been little traction for the bulls," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. The expected additional supply from OPEC+ is balancing potential for disruptions from sanctions, and while U.S. shale growth is expected to stall, U.S. production "does not appear to have slowed yet," he adds. WTI settles down 0.2% at $60.79 a barrel, July Brent goes off the board at $63.90, down 0.4%, while the most-active August contract falls 0.9% to $62.78 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1738 GMT - Rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by four to 461, a fifth straight weekly decline to the lowest level in three-and-a-half years, according to data from oil services company Baker Hughes. "Producers have been scaling back due to low prices and the increasing cost of steel," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. The drop in rig counts raises concerns about U.S. oil production falling, he says. Crude production averaged just under 13.5 million barrels a day in March, according to the latest EIA monthly data. Weekly estimates show output around 13.4 million b/d in May. "Shale producers are still nimble and can produce more with less, but make no mistake about it, this drop in rig counts signals that we're probably getting close to a solid bottom for oil," Flynn adds. Rigs directed at natural gas edged up by one this week to 99, or one less than a year ago.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1315 GMT - Oil and gas will be needed as energy sources and for the manufacturing of essential products for decades to come, Repsol's Chairman Antonio Brufau said Friday. Europe lacks a strategic plan for the transition away from refined fossil fuels, he said. "More than 95% of passenger and freight transport in Europe is powered by liquid fuels produced in refineries," he added. Brufau called for a reduced tax regime for renewable fuels, saying it would facilitate investment and boost consumption. Shares trade up 0.9% at 11.9 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1308 GMT - Repsol's chairman is calling on Europe to use pragmatism when developing energy transition policies in order to avoid harming competitiveness. Speaking at the Spanish oil company's annual general meeting, Antonio Brufau says that Europe needs to create a true energy union, and that it currently relies too much on Russia and China for energy. It makes no sense for Europe to pursue decarbonization strategies in isolation from others, especially when its carbon-dioxide emissions are significantly lower than China and the U.S., he says. Repsol will continue to invest oil and natural gas, as well as new renewable fuels, he adds. Shares trade up 0.7% at 11.87 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1305 GMT - Oil futures are lower with the market widely expecting OPEC+ to raise its output targets by another 411,000 barrels in July. The bearish factors that have driven the selloff this year, including tariffs and OPEC+ supply, may be mostly priced in by now, analysts at Citi say in a note. Oil has found support in "geopolitical risks vis-à-vis Iran and Russia-Ukraine, still-to-be-replenished low inventories, and firm refined product demand and cracks." The lack of significant increase in OPEC+ exports despite the accelerated unwinding of cuts has bolstered the view that the market might not turn as loose as previously expected, while Middle Eastern countries are building crude inventory for increased fuel use during the summer cooling season, they say. WTI is down 1.1% at $60.29, and Brent for August delivery is down 1.2% at $62.59 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1538 GMT - End markets are too soft for BRP to see a good runway to growth. CIBC's Mark Petrie says in a report that the leisurecraft maker remains hurt by excess industry inventory and cautious consumer demand that skews away from its strengths. While management is trying to turn things around with new products and innovation, "we ultimately see a broader demand recovery as the key to more normalized margins and a healthier valuation, and believe this will take time." Meanwhile, tariffs remain a headwind, but these are easing, Petrie says. Still, looking at the broader market, peer inventory levels remain elevated, and non-current units continue to outperform because of the elevated discount programs in place. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1417 GMT - United Airlines and JetBlue Airways' partnership has a lot to like, TD Cowen's Tom Fitzgerald and Helane Becker say in a research note. The arrangement will allow the airlines' customers to earn and spend frequent-flier miles on both airlines, among other benefits. "Miles in JetBlue's loyalty program become a more relevant currency with United's network while United gets a beachhead in JFK and increased access to Boston and Florida," say the analysts. They view this as a negative for both Delta Air Lines and American Airlines, but more so the latter given it was already challenged in the Northeast corridor. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1358 GMT - United Airlines and JetBlue Airways both benefit from their partnership given the complementary nature of their respective footprints, UBS' Thomas Wadewitz says in a research note. The arrangement will allow the airlines' customers to earn and spend frequent-flier miles on both airlines, among other benefits. The analyst says the biggest positive for JetBlue would be getting access to United's vast network, which could make its loyalty program more attractive as its customers could potentially use points from the program to fly United to markets not currently served by JetBlue. United gets a lift from its presence in JFK with seven round-trip slots beginning 2027, plus other hubs it doesn't currently have a strong footprint, adds Wadewitz. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1329 GMT - BRP's inventory headwinds are easing, opening the doors to growth and expansion. TD Cowen's Brian Morrison says that the leisurecraft manufacturer's inventory getting back on track will provide a path to revenue growth and margin expansion, and ultimately support market-share gains. Lower volumes could help boost margin, coupled with lower promotional intensity and product mix. TD upgrades its recommendation to buy from hold, even though there is still a cautious outlook on near-term consumer demand. "The cadence of recovery remains uncertain, but we have visibility to trough earnings that no longer warrants a trough multiple," Morrison says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1258 GMT - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1014 GMT - Li Auto looks set to regain positive sales momentum on product refreshes, Bernstein analysts write in a note. Although the company's 1Q results were largely in line with expectations, it provided positive 2Q delivery guidance of 123,000-128,000 units, implying sales of between 45,000 and 47,000 units each in May and June, they add. The revamped Li Mega has a strong order backlog, and production capacity is being ramped up to achieve monthly deliveries of 2,500-3,000 units by July, the analysts say. They also note that Li Auto is receiving more than 10,000 new orders weekly for the updated full-electric MPV and is confident it can return to monthly deliveries of 50,000 units soon. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0938 GMT - Li Auto's 2025 total sales remain challenging, Nomura analysts write in a note. The company's first-quarter earnings were largely in line with expectations and it and guided for 123,000-128,000 units of total shipments for the second quarter. With Li Auto's L series updates and efforts on promotions, the company should be able to maintain existing sales volumes despite an increase in the number of competitive new models in the market, they say. However, since the company doesn't have a plan for new hybrid plug-in models this year, the brokerage remains cautious on Li Auto's 2025 shipment growth. If the new full-electric SUVs are welcomed by the market, that could be a major growth opportunity for the company by the end of this year, Nomura says.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE prices down adjusted in Southeast Asia while quoting steady in other parts of the Asian region. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back on its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, heightened maintenance initiatives have effectively maintained overall supply pressure; nonetheless, seasonal demand remains below previous years, as purchasing power in downstream sectors continues to decrease. As a result, supply and demand are tightly connected, resulting in variations in the polyethylene market that is marked by a subdued quality. The slow demand has blocked price recovery, prompting traders to apply discounts to boost sales, even though transaction volumes have not risen notably. Although the import window is formally open for various categories, real USD-based offers and transactions are still considerably restricted. The lack of high domestic prices and lukewarm market sentiment have diminished foreign suppliers' eagerness to offer quotes to China. Fresh import opportunities are limited, and the ones that have appeared come with comparatively high costs. Considering the present weak RMB market, importers are demonstrating little interest in involvement. In the following week, elevated import expenses are expected to keep dissuading importers, likely causing a decline in the center of gravity of China's USD-price polyethylene market. In Southeast Asia, discussions regarding the import price of polyethylene (PE) experienced some support this week, driven by optimism that the recent trade concessions between the US and China could improve bilateral trade relations. Several prominent producers from the Middle East disclosed shipment proposals for June at elevated rates compared to the previous week, with transactions ultimately concluding within the assessed price ranges. The noticeable shortage of competitively priced shipments sourced from the US contributed to a rise in PE import negotiations, particularly in Vietnam, where prices for LLDPE and HDPE films demonstrated stronger evaluations. The LLDPE sector witnessed an uptick in deal-making and pricing discussions, primarily influenced by the limited availability of affordable US material. Conversely, the sentiment in the HDPE film market remained subdued as local producers intensified their efforts, increasing competition and exerting pressure on prices. The intensified competition among Southeast Asian suppliers resulted in a significant narrowing of the price gap between dutiable and non-dutiable PE imports. Although the slight improvement in sentiment regarding June shipments was a positive development for suppliers, concerns about its sustainability persist. Market participants in Southeast Asia continue to exercise caution, particularly with the impending conclusion of the 90-day suspension on US tariffs, set for July 9. In India, the sentiment in the Polyethylene (PE) market in India stayed mostly soft to stable this week, indicating minimal buying activity since most purchases for May shipments have already been completed. Purchasers mainly took a wait-and-see stance, expecting new price signals for June shipments�especially from suppliers in the Middle East�within the next week. Price trends differed among PE grades: HDPE and LLDPE stayed stable, whereas LDPE experienced a minor drop. Market dynamics were additionally affected by the expected restart of operations by a significant regional producer in early June, after a maintenance-induced shutdown. Furthermore, low netbacks in Southeast Asia might prompt Middle East suppliers to shift shipments to India, possibly worsening the existing oversupply. As seasonal demand declines in June�particularly from the food and beverage packaging sector�purchasers are anticipated to restrict buying to meet only immediate requirements. Numerous converters persist in functioning with low inventory levels, being wary because of the continuing sluggish market conditions. Nonetheless, a buoyed market sentiment in China, influenced by the recent 90-day tariff reprieve, might facilitate a slight increase in restocking efforts in the short term. In Pakistan, the PE market experienced a short rise in restocking activity as certain buyers began to restock inventories. This last-minute purchasing offered a short-term boost in a generally quiet market. The demand for HDPE film continues to be considerably weak in anticipation of the scheduled implementation of a single-use plastic bag prohibition in Sindh province, which is set to begin in June. This forthcoming regulatory adjustment is impacting sentiment and buying levels. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, in spite of with stable prices, overall buying activity remained quite low. Players were mainly reluctant or wary, opting to remain on the sidelines instead of participating in new deals. The main reason for this careful strategy was their expectation of future import proposals from foreign suppliers. These anticipated future offers were anticipated to affect market conditions or pricing, causing participants to refrain from making immediate purchases until clearer or more advantageous import terms emerged. In general, this position of caution illustrates a wait-and-observe strategy influenced by anticipations of forthcoming supply offers from global producers. LDPE: This week, Low density polyethylene prices witnessed a drop in parts of Asia. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back on its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, LDPE market activity continues to be sluggish even with attempts to manage supply by implementing longer maintenance turnarounds. These planned outages have proven successful in avoiding an oversupply scenario, especially as producers strive to stabilize inventory levels. Nevertheless, seasonal demand remains below historical averages, primarily because of diminished purchasing power in crucial downstream sectors like packaging and consumer products. This vulnerable demand situation has created a subtle equilibrium between supply and demand, leading to a mostly subdued market atmosphere. The absence of robust buying momentum has hindered any significant price recovery, prompting traders to rely more on price cuts to boost sales. However, these reductions in price have shown little effect on real transaction volumes, indicating buyer hesitation and weakened end-user demands. On the supply side, changes to operations are being implemented. Significantly, Sinopec Yanshan has begun maintenance activities at its LDPE and HDPE plants, which is anticipated to slowly constrain regional supply in the short term. Although this might provide some assistance to market fundamentals, a notable increase in prices is improbable unless demand conditions improve more clearly. In Southeast Asia, discussions on LDPE import prices received some backing this week, lifted by renewed hope after recent trade concessions between China and the US. Market players expressed optimism that enhanced bilateral relations might facilitate trade flows and possibly enhance regional demand. Nonetheless, this positivity was moderated by ongoing issues, as seasonal demand still lags behind past years and consumer purchasing power continues to be frail. Heightened competition among Southeast Asian suppliers additionally influenced market dynamics, resulting in a noticeable compression of the price gap between dutiable and non-dutiable PE imports. This compression indicates increasing pressure on suppliers to remain competitive in the face of weak purchasing interest. Although sentiment regarding June shipment inquiries has seen a slight enhancement�providing some comfort to sellers�market players continue to be cautious. Doubt hangs over the viability of this increase, particularly with the impending conclusion of the 90-day halt on US tariffs, scheduled to end on July 9. Purchasers are anticipated to adopt a cautious approach in their buying strategies until more definitive guidance appears regarding trade policies and regional demand patterns. As prices decline in Far East Asia (FEA) and Southeast Asia (SEA), mainly because of ongoing trade tariff uncertainties, international suppliers have turned their attention to South Asia for improved netbacks. This change has resulted in a significant rise in volume offers to the area, especially from exporters in the Middle East. Saudi suppliers, especially, have offered significant discounts on LDPE and PP grades to increase sales due to weak regional fundamentals in other areas. Even with the arrival of appealingly priced shipments, buyers in South Asia are wary, restricting their restocking efforts as demand from downstream sectors remains weak. Converters are still functioning at lower run rates, with many opting to take a wait-and-see stance until more distinct signals arise from the demand side. Simultaneously, challenges associated with logistics are increasing pressures on landed costs. Shipping costs from China to India have increased significantly, propelled by a lack of containers and growing congestion at key Chinese ports. These elements are exacerbating the cost situation and might counterbalance some of the price advantages provided by international vendors. In Pakistan, the LDPE market experienced a short rise in restocking efforts, as certain buyers entered after weeks of hesitation due to weak demand patterns in Asia. This last-minute purchase gave a fleeting boost to an otherwise quiet market, with participants observing that the momentum was brief and mainly influenced by urgent inventory requirements instead of a wider change in sentiment. The LDPE market in Sri Lanka and Bangladesh remained stable, with participants adopting a wait-and-see approach amid a lack of fresh import offers from overseas suppliers. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices slipped in parts of the Asian region. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back on its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, the LLDPE market has seen enhanced maintenance activities that have assisted in controlling overall supply pressure. Nonetheless, seasonal demand is still lower than in past years, as downstream purchasing power keeps declining. Consequently, supply and demand stay finely balanced, leading the polyethylene market to persistently exhibit fluctuations with a gentle undertone. Weak demand has constrained any significant price rebound, leading traders to provide price reductions to increase sales. However, transaction volumes have not yet demonstrated meaningful enhancement. While the import window is formally available for numerous product categories, genuine USD-denominated offers and transactions continue to be scarce. Low domestic prices and a lacklustre market sentiment have deterred foreign suppliers from making active quotes to China. New import offers are limited, and the few that have appeared are priced on the higher side. Due to the present weakness in the RMB market, importers are displaying minimal interest in participating. In the coming week, high import expenses are anticipated to keep buyers on the sidelines, probably causing the center of gravity of China's USD-denominated polyethylene market to decline. In Southeast Asia, negotiations on LLDPE import prices gained minor support despite an overall decrease in prices, bolstered by the optimism that recent trade concessions between the US and China could improve bilateral trade ties. Multiple leading Middle Eastern producers disclosed June shipment prices that exceeded those from the previous week, with deals ultimately closed within the assessed price ranges. The considerable shortage of competitively priced shipments from the US exerted upward pressure on LLDPE import discussions�particularly in Vietnam, where LLDPE film prices were evaluated as lower. The LLDPE sector experienced reduced transaction activity and heightened price negotiations, mainly because of the limited availability of affordable US resources. Conversely, sentiment within the LLDPE film market remained weak as domestic producers expanded their activities, intensifying competition and exerting downward pressure on costs. The heightened competition in the region led to a significant decrease in the price gap between dutiable and non-dutiable LLDPE imports. Despite the small improvement in sentiment regarding June shipments being a positive development for suppliers, doubts remain about its duration. Actors in the Southeast Asian market are remaining alert, particularly as they await the outcomes of the 90-day halt on US tariffs. In India, the sentiment in the LLDPE market stayed mostly stable to soft, indicating a lack of strong buying interest since most purchases for May shipments have been finalized. Purchasers mostly adopted a wait-and-see tactic, hoping for new pricing signals for June deliveries, especially from Middle Eastern vendors anticipated to release proposals in the next week. Price trends differed among polyethylene grades: LLDPE stayed consistent, whereas LDPE saw a minor decrease. Market dynamics were additionally influenced by the expected resumption of a significant regional producer in early June after a maintenance break. Moreover, low netbacks in Southeast Asia might encourage Middle Eastern suppliers to shift shipments to India, possibly exacerbating the current oversupply. As seasonal demand is anticipated to decline in June, particularly from the food and beverage packaging industry, buyers are probably going to limit purchases to immediate needs. Numerous converters persist in functioning with limited stock, being cautious due to the continuing weak market circumstances. Nonetheless, an enhancement in market sentiment in China, driven by the recent 90-day tariff reprieve, may aid in fostering a slight increase in restocking efforts shortly. In Pakistan, the LLDPE market saw a temporary decline in restocking efforts, even though there was a brief increase as certain buyers began to refill their stocks. This spur-of-the-moment purchasing provided merely short-lived assistance in an otherwise lacklustre market. The demand for LLDPE film is significantly low prior to the implementation of new packaging regulations, affecting both consumer sentiment and purchase quantities. Moreover, fluctuations in currency and persistent import limitations have also added to the decline, leading buyers to be reluctant to engage in significant volume transactions. The LLDPE market in Sri Lanka and Bangladesh was reportedly stable to soft as participants awaited fresh import offers from overseas suppliers. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices fell in Far East Asia and Southeast Asia, while they remained steady in South Asia. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, the supply of polypropylene (PP) is presently abundant, with producers mainly concentrating on reducing inventory. The market has entered a seasonal slowdown, and demand remains weak, resulting in a muted trading environment. The majority of restocking before the Dragon Boat Festival has been finalized, resulting in the market depending on soft, fundamental rigid demand. Even though certain production plants have restarted their activities, the total daily yield continues to be constrained because of the lasting effects of previous closures. This has aided in avoiding a sharper decline in PP prices, even with the lack of strong demand. As the month comes to an end, upstream petrochemical manufacturers are proactively working to deplete their inventories, while the majority of traders have finalized their sales strategies, moving stock to intermediate channels. Nonetheless, downstream demand continues to be weak, as falling order volumes do not offer significant price support. Spot prices have consistently declined, and the overall market sentiment stays cautious, as buyers primarily limit purchases to urgent, just-in-time requirements. Polypropylene (PP) prices decreased throughout Southeast Asia, as the export prospects for Chinese-sourced PP to the region are diminishing. Increasing container shipping costs, alongside steady Chinese PP FOB prices and weak market conditions in Southeast Asia, are diminishing arbitrage profits. In Indonesia, a domestic manufacturer upheld competitive PP prices, rendering new imports less feasible in the short term. Consequently, local producers are modifying export quantities to different markets like the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam to seek better profits. The brief halt of US reciprocal tariffs on exports from Southeast Asia will end on July 8. With time running out, Southeast Asian converters have a limited opportunity to ship goods to the US in a timely manner, causing uncertainties regarding new export orders. Regional converters persist in voicing worries regarding the short-term demand outlook. The possible reintroduction of US tariffs is anticipated to adversely affect export demand, putting strain on resin purchases and operational levels. Moreover, the bleak economic forecast for the region in 2025 is raising worries, putting additional pressure on the total offtake of PP throughout Southeast Asia. In India, prices for PP homopolymer have remained stable this week since trading activity has diminished, with the majority of May shipments already confirmed. Likewise, block copolymer prices remained stable, staying close to four-month lows. Most buyers are adopting a wait-and-see stance, looking forward to new offers for June shipments, especially from Middle Eastern suppliers anticipated next week. A key domestic producer, previously offline for maintenance, is set to resume operations in early June. Nonetheless, certain sources with clients primarily in the window profile and pipe sectors indicated that purchasing interest was a bit slower than in April. May has not seen the expected seasonal demand from the construction sector, and there's mixed sentiment regarding the level of this demand for June, which is usually a robust month for PVC consumption. In the downstream piping and window framing sector, sources indicated that there was fierce rivalry among manufacturers. Producers have observed that demand this year has not been as weak as anticipated, holding a somewhat more positive outlook than what some other market players have mentioned, but the overall scenario remains subdued. Buying interest in the spot market was stronger in Poland compared to other regions in Europe. Supply in Europe continues to be adequate. A facility in Germany continues with scheduled maintenance for PVC manufacturing. Although production at facilities in Poland and France had recently experienced disruptions, they were planning to resume operations by the end of May. Asian materials keep arriving in Europe, with a changing perspective from certain smaller converters regarding their acceptance. It was reported that materials from China were being offered at the most competitive rates. Producers will remain eager to restore margins in June, as demand for PVC typically reaches its peak in the upcoming months. Market players focused on the ethylene industry-settled contract price for June. Producers noted the necessity to enhance their margins amid any shift in ethylene, pointing to present producer margins as unviable. The market monitored closely the progress in peace talks between Russia and Ukraine, as a resolution could result in heightened demand for reconstruction activities. In other regions, a fluctuating tariff environment was noted to reduce demand by fostering economic instability. In the meantime, E-PVC demand stays weak, the automotive industry is sluggish in Europe, and although exported quantities offer some optimism, ongoing tariff disputes from the US have caused concern among certain domestic producers in Europe. The US has currently imposed a 10% tariff on e-PVC imports from the European Union, but there was a potential escalation on Friday when President Trump proposed a universal 50% tariff on European exports to the US starting June 1. The tariff proposal may serve as an additional negotiation strategy to facilitate a deal, yet in the near term, the ambiguity will pose difficulties for everyone in terms of planning and management. The US has restricted domestic production of e-PVC, leading to a dependence on imports. It was suggested that American buyers will keep acquiring e-PVC from Europe since alternatives are quite limited, though 50% tariffs would pose challenges. In Turkey, market activity declined as Eid al-Adha drew near. European producers maintained a limited desire to export to Turkey at the current price points, due to competitive offers from other nations. Certain offers from China were withdrawn due to anticipated rises in shipping costs from Asia to Turkey. PET: This week, PET prices displayed mix undertone in the European region. The European Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) market experienced a varied price trend, influenced by changing market dynamics throughout the months. The PET spot market experienced an increase in spot prices due to higher offers surfacing from Asia. Market players observed that prices climbed this week, especially for June delivery, as offers from Asia surged due to elevated shipping expenses to Europe. The European Union Commission declared an antidumping inquiry into Vietnamese PET, which was also noted to exert a bullish pressure on prices. Consequently, European players have displayed a heightened interest in domestic products, a trend that sources anticipate will persist into June. Prices in Europe have risen, particularly for June delivery. Although May saw restricted sales volumes, due to rising supply from Asia and the ongoing antidumping inquiry, there is heightened demand for European products currently. Supply availability for PET throughout Europe remained plentiful, with no difficulties obtaining extra volumes. Nevertheless, this week, an increased demand for feedstock paraxylene and purified terephthalic acid was observed. The preform season has also been reported as remaining weak, with sources anticipating a late surge in demand from preform producers. PET contract prices fell, mainly because of weak downstream demand and cautious purchasing in the bottle and food packaging sectors. Although feedstock prices (PTA and MEG) remained stable, low consumer confidence and reduced end-user activity limited further price increases. Adding to this, logistical idest export operations restricted buying interest, resulting in a weaker price climate. Sufficient supply, backed by stable production and elevated inventory levels, satisfied consistent yet modest demand. Simultaneously, reduced procurement activity and growing import availability applied minor downward pressure, leading to a decline in prices by month's end, mirroring a cautiously balanced market scenario. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices assessed stable in the US. US spot export HDPE prices remained stable for all grades this week, as market sentiment continued to be mixed. Despite recent tariff-related changes offering brief respite, the overall demand still showed uncertainty, leading buyers to stay hesitant without a significant downstream recovery. Market participants were looking forward to new June offers, anticipated to be announced in the next few days. Although production delays in April and May briefly constrained supply, they had little effect on enhancing overall demand. As operations have mostly stabilized in June, focus is shifting to whether market fundamentals will change sufficiently to enable any possible price variation. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices were assessed steady in the US. Spot export activity for Low density polyethylene (LDPE) on an FAS Houston basis continued to be slow over the week, mainly because of poor demand. Market participants noted that pickup times have been delayed due to port congestion and a lack of containers at terminals in Houston. Even though warehouse backlogs are likely to diminish shortly, packing operations persist, as shipment arrivals are still postponed. Certain sources reported delays reaching three weeks, which added pressure on logistics and slowed down the pace of export activities. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices quoted flat in the US. US spot export LLDPE prices held steady across all grades for the week, despite varying market sentiment. Although recent tariff developments provided brief respite, overall demand continues to be unstable, with buyers taking a cautious stance due to the lack of clear signs of downstream recovery. PP: This week, PP export prices rolled over in the US. US spot export polypropylene (PP) prices stayed unchanged throughout the week, with market activity decelerating as participants anticipated the May contract settlement for polymer-grade propylene (PGP). Negotiations are in progress, with views divided�some expect a continuation from 38 cents/lb in April, while others predict a drop because of ongoing weak downstream demand. The result of the PGP contract is seen as an essential directional signal for the PP market. If PGP declines, market indications imply that US PP manufacturers might lower their prices in response. PS: This week, PS export prices were assessed stable in the US. US polystyrene (PS) prices remained steady to modestly reduced over the week. Market activity in South America continued to be restricted. A distributor in Brazil observed that they had not managed considerable PS volumes in the last three months, pointing to low demand and sufficient domestic supply. The low regional prices diminished the motivation to participate in further trading activities. PVC: This week, PVC export prices left unchanged in the US. Fresh June offer announcements from a US producer have been made for the PVC spot export market, showing a USD 40/mt rise compared to May prices. During ongoing negotiations, prices stayed steady throughout the week as market players looked for confirmed transactions. Various factors are influencing the global PVC market. The US has been facing weak demand and ongoing price pressure from competitively priced imports from China. Nevertheless, various factors are driving expectations of possible price increases�such as the impending BIS certification deadline in India, escalating freight costs, Brazil's recent anti-dumping decision, and increased June proposals from Asian providers. These factors are introducing complexity to market sentiment as buyers and sellers maneuver through short-term price trends. PET: This week, PET prices rolled over in the US. Activity in the US spot import market for Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) remained limited throughout the week, impacted by increasing ocean freight costs and prolonged transit times. These logistical issues have deterred importers from active trading while increasing their cost pressures for importers. Domestic contract price settlements for feedstocks, Paraxylene (PX) and Purified terephthalic acid (PTA), have been done for March, April, and May. In major Southeast Asian markets, exporters noted weak inquiries and slow demand, indicating a wider decline in global PET trade activity.

data.title: China bristles at Macron linking Ukraine defence to Taiwan threats
data.description:
China on Saturday criticised as a "double standard" attempts to link the defence of Ukraine with the need to protect Taiwan from a Chinese invasion - a thinly veiled reference to a speech by French Emmanuel Macron in Singapore on Friday night.

As part of a broader address on the risks of division between China and the United States, Macron told the Shangri-La Dialogue defence meeting that if Russia was allowed to take any part of Ukraine without constraint then "what could happen in Taiwan?".

In a Facebook post, China's embassy in Singapore said that comparing the Taiwan issue with the Ukraine issue is "unacceptable".

"The two are different in nature and not comparable at all," the post said, saying that Taiwan was entirely an internal affair for China.

"If one tries to denounce a 'double standard' with a double standard, the only result we can get is still a double standard," the post said.

The embassy post did not mention Macron directly but it was accompanied by a photo of him talking at the event.

Beijing has previously dispatched defence ministers and other senior military officials to the annual meeting, which ends Sunday, but this year sent a relatively low-level delegation of military academics.

China views democratically-governed Taiwan as its own territory and has stepped up military and political pressure to assert those claims, including increasing the intensity of war games, saying the island is one of its provinces with no right to be called a state.

Taiwan's government rejects Beijing's sovereignty claims, saying only the island's people can decide their future.

U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the gathering on Saturday that China posed an "imminent" threat and any attempt to conquer Taiwan "would result in devastating consequences for the Indo-Pacific and the world".

Regional diplomats said that Macron's comments were far from isolated during the freewheeling, informal meeting and risks of a Russian victory emboldening a Chinese invasion of Taiwan had at times surfaced in sideline discussions.

"The message from many backing Ukraine is that the line must be held if a message is to be sent to China," said one East Asian envoy.

data.title: Elon Musk's Smashup of the SEC Won't Be Soon Forgotten — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Martin Kimel

About the author: Martin Kimel is an attorney who recently retired from the Securities and Exchange Commission. He is the co-author of The Pessimist's Son: A Holocaust Memoir of Hope.

For nearly 31 years, I was what President Donald Trump would call a "corrupt, incompetent and unnecessary deep state bureaucrat." The Securities and Exchange Commission was my professional home under leadership appointed by both parties. Before my retirement this year, I watched with alarm as the agency was being damaged by people who haven't the vaguest clue what public service means.

My ex-colleagues still at the Commission can't speak out in their own defense. But I can. Cuts to the SEC, spearheaded by Elon Musk before he departed from the U.S. DOGE Service this week, won't save money. But they will put investors at greater risk and may slow the pace of financial innovation.

The SEC's lawyers, accountants, economists, and experts oversee the nation's $120 trillion securities markets. They deter fraud, ensure fair and efficient markets, and help businesses access capital. Virtually any of them could get higher-paying private-sector jobs. Instead they chose to devote themselves to the unglamorous work of serving the public.

The approximately 4,200 SEC employees (5,000, until recently) are decidedly not "low productivity," regardless of what Musk might think. They enforce federal securities laws, approve products, review corporate filings, examine broker-dealers and registered investment advisors, write and amend rules, provide guidance to the industry, and educate the public.

I worked through Trump's first term, but that still didn't prepare me for how bad things would get under his second.

The Trump administration, Musk, and DOGE didn't take long to come for us. DOGE stands for Department of Government Efficiency — but it isn't a department and it certainly hasn't been making anyone at the SEC more efficient. Like other federal agencies, we were required to submit the lists of five things we did last week. There is no apparent use for these lists, making them the very definition of government waste.

Although telework at the SEC was a huge success, Trump ordered us back to the office five days a week — a stricter policy than we had before Covid-19. Trump touted the policy as "a way to encourage people to quit." No business encourages its employees — regardless of what they do and how well they do it — to quit en masse. Yet, thanks to DOGE, this is what happened.

The administration then abruptly announced, without consulting SEC leadership, that it was canceling the leases of the SEC's Los Angeles and Philadelphia offices. If less-expensive office space wasn't found before the leases were terminated, the staff was informed, they would work every day from home. So much for return-to-office efficiency.

I experienced some dark times during my SEC career, including the 2008-09 financial crisis and the Enron and Madoff scandals. But morale at the Commission is the worst I have ever seen — by far. No job is secure. Nobody knows what will become of the agency or its independence.

So when the SEC offered early retirement and an incentive payment for people to voluntarily resign, I and hundreds of others reluctantly accepted. Many co-workers told me that they wished they, too, qualified for retirement or could afford to retire. It was heartbreaking. In addition, many excellent mid-career professionals quickly found private-sector jobs and left. More employees will follow.

As talented as the remaining SEC employees are, I hear from some still at the agency that they are struggling to get the work done. That isn't surprising, given the sudden exodus of so many experienced folks. Moreover, weakening the examinations and enforcement divisions, and others, may result in more scams and fraud going undetected or un-prosecuted. Americans' investments toward a home, college, or retirement will be less protected.

The Commission will probably also make more mistakes. It is a highly collaborative organization, with all draft rules and enforcement recommendations reviewed by various offices and policy divisions, such as Investment Management, Trading and Markets, and Corporation Finance. Those reviews may now not always be of the same high quality (or speed).

Finally, the SEC staff works closely with industry to facilitate innovation, and this, too, may suffer. SEC staff frequently work with a financial product's sponsor to craft conditions in exemptive orders so that a product can be brought to market, albeit more safely for investors. For instance, exchange traded funds didn't exist before 1993 and required various exemptive orders from the SEC. Since their launch, ETFs have evolved and grown explosively, with total net assets of $10.3 trillion by 2025. With less-expert and fewer SEC staff, the pace of product innovation may slow.

And what will be gained? Shrinking the SEC's footprint and slashing its head count won't save taxpayers a dime. The agency's annual budget of about $2.1 billion doesn't contribute to the federal deficit, because the Commission effectively pays for itself through industry fees.

Government jobs used to entail a certain bargain: You made less money, but you got to wear a "white hat" and do interesting and important work — with job security. By breaking this bargain, Musk and the Trump administration have, perhaps permanently, made government service less attractive to the most talented workers. That is an odd and unfortunate result for people who claim to be improving the federal government.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
